when: called by external code (not just the same app)
reason: the public API becomes a maintenance contract

when: the external caller is a plugin or extension
reason: core must depend on interfaces only

when: business rules exist even without a database
reason: rules independent of persistence deserve a domain layer

when: mostly moving data in and out with validation
reason: a domain model would cost more than it saves

when: the data movement IS the point (transform, not serve)
reason: structure follows stages, not layers

when: asynchronous messages cross process boundaries
reason: eventual consistency changes every structural rule

when: none of the above; one-off execution
reason: no callers means no contract to protect

when: single file, single concern, no callers beyond the immediate task
reason: ceremony where it doesn't earn its keep — just write it correctly; note 'this is a script; flat is fine'

when: torn between data-centric and domain-rich
reason: start one archetype simpler: upgrading is a straightforward extraction, downgrading is dismantling dead weight

when: about to add a layer
then: name the independent change that layer contains — or drop the layer
reason: structure exists to contain change (Parnas 1972); a layer that never repays itself in a future change was a mistake

when: about to apply Repository/Strategy/Factory/Observer
then: require three concrete cases today — or one case plus a confirmed second in the visible horizon
reason: rule of three; forty years of 'someday' abstractions never paid rent (YAGNI)

when: one person will maintain all of this code
reason: Conway's Law: boundaries protect team seams; with one owner they protect nothing

when: the system spans multiple archetypes
then: classify each component separately and name the boundary between them
reason: do not force one archetype onto the whole system

when: choosing where module boundaries go
then: each module hides exactly one design decision likely to change
reason: Parnas's test: if you can't name the hidden decision, the module doesn't earn its existence

when: building UI screens or views (web or mobile)
reason: rendering as a pure function of state is the settled idiom across React/Redux, Elm, SwiftUI, and Compose

when: UI grown to many features needing team-scale partitioning
reason: vertical feature slices with public APIs keep teams and workers from colliding

when: building a reusable UI component library or design tokens
reason: the primitive layer must stay ignorant of every consuming app

when: multiple independently deployable services over a network
reason: independent deploys require exclusive data ownership and published contracts

when: an LLM drives decisions through tool calls in a host loop
reason: the host stays generic; intelligence lives in the model and the tool registry

when: an LLM decomposes work at runtime and dispatches sub-agents
reason: isolation between workers is what makes runtime decomposition safe

when: declaring infrastructure or deployment desired state (Terraform/K8s)
reason: environment-agnostic modules plus thin parameterization is the cross-tool consensus

when: ML features and models with separate training and serving paths
reason: training/serving skew is the defining failure; one shared transform path prevents it

when: realtime simulation with many entity types sharing behaviors
reason: composition over inheritance at data-layout granularity

when: tempted to split command and query models (CQRS)
then: apply only inside one bounded context with measured asymmetric read/write load — never as the whole-app default
reason: Fowler and Azure guidance both warn against broad CQRS; it refines event-driven, it does not replace it

when: stateful concurrent entities exchanging messages in-process (actor style)
then: apply event-driven rules at actor granularity: one actor type per worker, freeze message protocols
reason: the actor model is event-driven with per-entity state ownership
